30	---
31	
32	# DAG Walker
33	
34	You take a **seed declaration** and walk UP its dependency cone in the leandag graph, making the cone **complete**: every declaration the seed transitively depends on must have (1) a real statement block, (2) an informal proof so its effort is finite (not ∞), and (3) a `\uses{}` set that matches the dependencies its mathematics actually uses. You turn ∞-effort cones into finite ones and you make the frontier trustworthy — a node is only honestly "ready to prove" when its `\uses{}` is complete and every dependency is done or itself on the way.
35	
36	You are the dependency-completeness agent. Unlike a blueprint-writer (one chapter, precise directive), you follow the graph wherever it goes — across chapters — filling whatever is missing beneath the seed.
37	
38	## Directive Format
39	
40	```markdown
41	# DAG Walker Directive
42	
43	## Slug
44	<slug>
45	
46	## Seed
47	<the blueprint label to walk up from, e.g. thm:pic_zero_dimension_equals_genus.
48	 May be an ∞-source, the project goal, or a frontier node to make honest.>
49	
50	## Strategy context
51	<the slice of STRATEGY.md the dispatcher extracted — what this cone supports in
52	 the overall arc. You do NOT read STRATEGY.md yourself.>
53	
54	## Depth / scope
55	<how far up to walk: "the whole cone to the axioms" (default), or "one level —
56	 the seed's direct \uses only", or "stop at chapter X". Plus any out-of-scope
57	 declarations.>
58	
59	## References
60	- <path/to/reference.md or pointer>: <which results live here>
61	```
62	
63	## How you walk
64	
65	Use **`archon dag-query`** (read-only) to navigate — do not eyeball the graph or trust memory:
66	
67	```
68	archon dag-query node      --node <seed> --json      # inspect the seed
69	archon dag-query ancestors --node <seed> --json      # the full dependency closure (everything it transitively uses)
70	archon dag-query gaps --json                         # all ∞ holes, project-wide
71	```
72	
73	`archon` is on PATH. JSON goes to stdout (the banner to stderr), so `--json` is parseable. For each node in the cone, check the **three completeness conditions** below, fix what's broken, and **recurse**: a block you add has its own dependencies — add those too, walking up until you bottom out at a done node (`\leanok` / `\mathlibok`) or an axiom (a node with no further dependencies).
74	
75	## The three completeness conditions (per node in the cone)
76	
77	1. **Exists with a statement.** Every `\uses{label}` in the cone must resolve to a real blueprint block. A `\uses{}` pointing at a label that exists in no chapter is a **broken edge** (`archon dag-query` / `leandag build --json` reports these). Fix it: correct the label, or — if the dependency is real but unblueprinted — **add the missing `\begin{lemma/definition}` block** (statement + `\label` + `\lean` + `\uses`) in the appropriate chapter.
78	
79	2. **Has an informal proof — finite effort, not ∞.** A node whose `effort_local` is ∞ is a statement with **no `\begin{proof}`** (and no sorry-free Lean): a roadmap hole. Formalizing it would be blind progress. Give it a proof sketch:
80	   - If the result is genuinely the project's to prove, **write the informal proof** (mathematical prose, project notation, `\uses{}` for each step's dependencies), grounded in a reference per citation discipline below.
81	   - If **Mathlib already provides it**, make it a **Mathlib dependency anchor** (`\mathlibok`, see below) instead of proving it.
82	   - If it is genuinely hard / not-yet-in-the-literature / needs novel mathematics, **do not fabricate a proof** — leave it ∞, record it under "Could not complete", and surface any strategy implication.
83	
84	3. **`\uses{}` is complete — the trust anchor.** Read the node's statement and informal proof, and verify that **every mathematical fact it actually relies on is declared in `\uses{}`**. If the proof of `T` invokes lemma `L` but `T` does not `\uses{lem:L}`, add it — and if `lem:L` has no blueprint block, create it (condition 1) and walk up into it. This is the single most important check: an under-declared `\uses{}` makes a node *look* ready (its missing dependency is invisible to the frontier) when its real foundation is not done. Do not over-declare either: a `\uses{}` to something the proof does not use is noise — remove it only when you are sure it is spurious.
85	
86	   **When the node has Lean code, the Lean side is the ground truth.** Read the matched `.lean` declaration (you read Lean; you never edit it): the facts its proof *actually* invokes — project lemmas it calls, instances it needs — are what `\uses{}` must transcribe, not just what the informal sketch happens to mention. And the project keeps a **1-to-1 Lean ↔ blueprint correspondence**: if the cone's Lean files contain helper declarations with no blueprint entry (`archon dag-query unmatched` lists them), give each one an entry — trivial statement, `\label{}`, `\lean{}`, accurate `\uses{}`, one-line proof — and wire its consumers to `\uses{}` it. A helper without tex is an invisible dependency.
87	
88	A node is **complete** when it is `\leanok` / `\mathlibok`, or it has a real statement + a finite-effort proof + a complete `\uses{}` whose targets are themselves complete or on the way. Stop walking up through `\mathlibok` anchors — they are the leaves of the proof-obligation tree.
89	
90	## Markers (same rules as every blueprint agent)
91	
92	- **Never add `\leanok`.** It is earned by a sorry-free Lean proof and set by the deterministic `sync_leanok` phase. Not yours.
93	- **`\mathlibok` only on a genuine Mathlib dependency anchor.** When the cone bottoms out at a result Mathlib already provides, write the statement, point `\lean{}` at the **real Mathlib declaration**, mark `\mathlibok`, and add a `\textit{Provided by Mathlib.}` line — no `\begin{proof}` needed. This gives leandag a done node so the `\uses{}` resolves and the effort is finite. **Anti-hallucination rule:** mark `\mathlibok` ONLY when the statement genuinely exists in Mathlib in the form you wrote. A wrong `\mathlibok` is worse than an ∞ hole — the loop skips proving a real gap. If unsure it is in Mathlib, leave it ∞ and report it.
94	
95	## Citation discipline (the hard rule)
96	
97	Every block you write that derives from external reference material needs: a `% SOURCE:` comment (`<pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`), a `% SOURCE QUOTE:` with the **verbatim** original-language statement copied from that local file, a `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` before the proof environment when applicable, and a visible `\textit{Source: <pointer>.}` first line. **Never write any of these from training memory.** If you lack the local source, spawn a `reference-retriever` (foreground Bash, blocking — needs `references/**` in your write-domain) and wait for it **inside this turn**: if the harness auto-backgrounds the dispatch and hands you a task ID, poll its report (`Monitor` on an until-loop; foreground `sleep` is blocked) until it lands — never end your turn assuming a still-running retriever returned. Then open the new `references/<slug>.md` and write the citation block. If retrieval fails, leave the block flagged and report it INCOMPLETE — never substitute a paraphrase or a remembered quote.
98	
99	## What you must / must not do
100	
101	- **Stay in your write-domain** (`blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`). The CLI rejects writes outside it. You do NOT edit `.lean` files, `content.tex`, `macros/`, or any state file. A needed macro is a "Notes" item.
102	- **Respect protected material** (the "Protected by the mathematician" section of your invocation prompt): never edit a protected chapter or an `all`-protected label's block; a `statement`-protected block may only gain a `proof` environment. Wiring INTO protected blocks is fine — other declarations may `\uses{}` their labels — but their own `\uses{}` lists are the mathematician's; report missing edges there in "Notes for dispatcher" instead of editing.
103	- **Keep every chapter valid LaTeX** (matched `\begin`/`\end`, balanced braces in `\label`/`\uses`/`\lean`).
104	- **Mathematical prose, not Lean syntax.** No tactics, no typeclass notes, no project-history narrative — the blueprint reads as a standalone document.
105	- **Re-query after editing.** Run `archon dag-query ancestors --node <seed>` again at the end: the cone should now have zero broken `\uses{}` and (except for declared "Could not complete" nodes) zero ∞ effort. Fix and re-check until it converges or only genuine-gap nodes remain.
106	
107	## Workflow
108	
109	1. Read the directive. Inspect the seed: `archon dag-query node --node <seed> --json`.
110	2. Pull the cone: `archon dag-query ancestors --node <seed> --json`. Note which nodes are ∞ (need a proof), which `\uses{}` are broken, and which blocks may be missing.
111	3. Read the relevant chapters on disk and `references/summary.md`; read every reference your directive names.
112	4. Walk the cone bottom-up (axioms/Mathlib first), applying the three checks. Add missing blocks, add missing `\uses{}` edges, write missing proofs or Mathlib anchors. Recurse into anything new you introduce.
113	5. For any block needing a source you don't have locally, spawn a `reference-retriever`, wait, read the file, then write the cited block.
114	6. Re-query the cone; fix residual broken edges / ∞ holes until only genuine gaps remain.
115	7. Write your report.
116	
117	## Logging
118	
119	Write your report to `.archon/task_results/dag-walker-<slug>.md` (or the nested `task_results/<parent-slug>/...` path your invocation names).
120	
121	```markdown
122	# DAG Walker Report
123	
124	## Slug
125	<slug>
126	
127	## Seed
128	<label>
129	
130	## Status
131	<COMPLETE — cone fully grounded | PARTIAL — genuine gaps remain (listed below)>
132	
133	## Cone before → after
134	- ∞ holes: <N before> → <M after>
135	- broken \uses: <N before> → <M after>
136	- blocks added: <count>;  \uses edges added: <count>
137	
138	## Blocks added / proofs written
139	- `\label{...}` in `chapters/<slug>.tex` — <one line: statement / why it was a missing dependency>
140	- Proof sketch for `<label>` — <brief shape; ∞ → finite>
141	- Mathlib anchor `<label>` → `\lean{<real Mathlib decl>}` `\mathlibok`
142	
143	## \uses edges added/fixed (the completeness fixes)
144	- `<node>` now `\uses{<dep>}` — its proof invokes <dep> but didn't declare it (made the frontier honest).
145	- Fixed broken `\uses{<bad>}` in `<node>` → `<good>` / created `<dep>`.
146	
147	## Could not complete (genuine gaps — strategy items)
148	- `<label>` — no informal proof exists (hard / novel / not in literature). Left ∞. <strategy implication, if any>
149	
150	## References consulted
151	- `references/<file>.md` — <what verbatim content you took>
152	
153	## Notes for dispatcher
154	- <cross-chapter concerns, needed macros, oversized chapters, etc.>
155	```
156	
157	## Return Value
158	
159	Your final assistant message: one line — `<slug>: COMPLETE | PARTIAL — <one-sentence outcome>` — plus the path to your full report. Keep it short; the dispatcher reads the report.

43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | read | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | **write** (shared notice board) | **write** (shared notice board) | **write** (shared notice board) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	Archon talks back to the user through **`TO_USER.md`** — a single, *persistent* notice board surfaced as a UI banner. It is **shared and writable by every agent** (plan, prover, review). It is no longer cleared at the start of each review phase; content survives across iters until an agent prunes it. The discipline every writer follows:
112	
113	- **Concise**: the whole file stays ≤ 2–3 short bullets. It is a banner, not a log.
114	- **Relevant now**: before adding anything, read the current content and **delete any bullet that is no longer true at this point** (the blocker was resolved, the decision was overtaken, the credential got set). Never let it accumulate stale notices.
115	- **Notice board, never a question queue**: the loop is autonomous and may run unattended for many iters. Never write "awaiting your decision", an options menu, a "where to reply", or "default to X if no reply" — anything implying the loop is paused for a human. State decisions as *made* and blockers as *worked-around-as-far-as-possible*; the user steers asynchronously via `USER_HINTS.md`.
116	
117	## Agent Roles
118	
119	### Plan Agent
120	
121	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
122	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
123	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
124	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
125	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
126	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
127	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
128	
129	### Prover Agent
130	
131	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
132	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
133	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
134	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
135	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
136	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
137	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
138	
139	### Subagents
140	
141	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
142	
143	### Review Agent
144	
145	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
146	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
147	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
148	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
149	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
150	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
151	
152	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
153	
154	These run automatically between phases:
155	
156	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
157	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
158	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.

######## Cohomology_HigherDirectImage.tex ########
\chapter{Higher direct images $R^i f_*$ of quasi-coherent sheaves ($i \geq 1$)}
\label{chap:Cohomology_HigherDirectImage}
% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/HigherDirectImage.lean
% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Tags 02KE
% (Quasi-coherence of higher direct images), 02KG (relative affine vanishing),
% and 02KH (Flat base change) (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex and
% references/stacks-coherent.md).

\section{Setup and motivation}

This chapter treats the higher derived direct images \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) for
\(i \geq 1\), complementing the \(i = 0\) (direct-image) case of
Chapter~\ref{chap:Cohomology_FlatBaseChange}. These higher direct images are the
single deepest foundation of the cohomology-and-base-change package: the
Cohen--Macaulay regularity bound, semicontinuity of fibre cohomology, and the
\(m\)-regularity estimate of FGA all rest on the basic finiteness and base-change
properties of \(R^i f_*\) recorded here.

We use the classical cohomology-sheaf formulation (not the derived category) and a
{\v C}ech-complex approach, parallel to the \(i = 0\) treatment of
Chapter~\ref{chap:Cohomology_FlatBaseChange}.

Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes that is quasi-compact and
quasi-separated, and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module.
A base change of \(f\) along a morphism \(g : S' \to S\) is recorded by the
cartesian square obtained from the fibre product \(X' = X \times_S S'\):
\[
  \begin{array}{ccc}
    X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
    {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
    S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
  \end{array}
\]
Here \(f' : X' \to S'\) is the base change of \(f\), \(g' : X' \to X\) is the first
projection, and \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) is the pullback of
\(\mathcal{F}\) to \(X'\).

\section{Definition of the higher direct images}

\begin{definition}\leanok
[Higher direct image]
  \label{def:higher_direct_image}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.higherDirectImage}
  % NOTE (iter-233): the Lean `higherDirectImage` carries an explicit instance
  % hypothesis `[HasInjectiveResolutions X.Modules]`, because Mathlib has
  % `Abelian X.Modules` but NOT `EnoughInjectives` / `IsGrothendieckAbelian` for
  % `SheafOfModules` over a sheaf of rings (the Grothendieck-abelian instance is
  % only for `Sheaf J A` with a fixed value category). The definition is therefore
  % conditional; the hypothesis can be dropped once a lane proves that instance.
  % The file is not yet imported by the aggregator (orphan module).
  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
  % `lemma-relative-affine-vanishing' (proof), describing $R^i f_*$ as the
  % $i$-th right derived functor of $f_*$ via the sheafified presheaf
  % $V \mapsto H^i(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)})$
  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L188--192).
  % SOURCE QUOTE: "According to
  % Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-describe-higher-direct-images}
  % the sheaf
  % $R^if_*\mathcal{F}$ is the sheaf associated to the presheaf
  % $V \mapsto H^i(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)})$."
  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, \S\,Quasi-coherence of
  higher direct images (and the underlying Cohomology chapter).}
  Let \(f : X \to S\) be a morphism of schemes and let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a
  quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. For \(i \geq 0\) the \emph{\(i\)-th
  higher direct image} \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) is the \(i\)-th right derived functor
  of the pushforward functor \(f_*\) applied to \(\mathcal{F}\). Concretely,
  \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) is the \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module obtained as the
  sheafification of the presheaf
  \[
    V \;\longmapsto\; H^i\bigl(f^{-1}(V),\, \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)}\bigr),
    \qquad V \subset S \text{ open},
  \]
  where \(H^i(-, -)\) denotes sheaf cohomology. For \(i = 0\) this recovers the
  ordinary pushforward \(R^0 f_*\mathcal{F} = f_*\mathcal{F}\).
\end{definition}

\section{Quasi-coherence of the higher direct images}

\begin{lemma}\leanok
[Quasi-coherence of higher direct images]
  \label{lem:higher_direct_image_quasi_coherent}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.higherDirectImage_isQuasiCoherent}
  \uses{def:higher_direct_image, lem:higher_direct_image_affine_vanishing}
  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Tag 02KE (Lemma
  % `lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images', part (1))
  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L741--757).
  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $f : X \to S$ be a morphism of schemes.
  % Assume that $f$ is quasi-separated and quasi-compact.
  % \begin{enumerate}
  % \item For any quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module $\mathcal{F}$ the
  % higher direct images $R^pf_*\mathcal{F}$ are quasi-coherent on $S$.
  % \item If $S$ is quasi-compact, there exists an integer $n = n(X, S, f)$
  % such that $R^pf_*\mathcal{F} = 0$ for all $p \geq n$ and any
  % quasi-coherent sheaf $\mathcal{F}$ on $X$.
  % \item In fact, if $S$ is quasi-compact we can find $n = n(X, S, f)$
  % such that for every
  % morphism of schemes $S' \to S$ we have $R^p(f')_*\mathcal{F}' = 0$
  % for $p \geq n$ and any quasi-coherent sheaf $\mathcal{F}'$
  % on $X'$. Here $f' : X' = S' \times_S X \to S'$ is the base change of $f$.
  % \end{enumerate}"
  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Tag 02KE (``Quasi-coherence of higher direct
  images''), part (1).}
  Let \(f : X \to S\) be a quasi-compact and quasi-separated morphism of schemes.
  Then for every quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module \(\mathcal{F}\) and every
  \(p \geq 0\), the higher direct image \(R^p f_*\mathcal{F}\) is a quasi-coherent
  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module.
\end{lemma}

% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "We first prove (1). Note that under the hypotheses of the
% lemma the sheaf
% $R^0f_*\mathcal{F} = f_*\mathcal{F}$ is quasi-coherent by
% Schemes, Lemma \ref{schemes-lemma-push-forward-quasi-coherent}.
% Using
% Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-localize-higher-direct-images}
% we see that forming higher direct images commutes with restriction
% to open subschemes. Since being quasi-coherent is local on $S$ we
% reduce to the case discussed in the next paragraph.
%
% Proof of (1) in case $S$ is affine. We will use the induction principle.
% Since $f$ quasi-compact and quasi-separated we see that $X$
% is quasi-compact and quasi-separated. For $U \subset X$
% quasi-compact open and $a = f|_U$ we let $P(U)$ be the property that
% $R^pa_*\mathcal{F}$ is quasi-coherent on $S$ for all quasi-coherent modules
% $\mathcal{F}$ on $U$ and all $p \geq 0$. Since $P(X)$ is (1), it suffices
% the prove conditions (1) and (2) of Lemma \ref{lemma-induction-principle} hold.
% If $U$ is affine, then $P(U)$ holds because $R^pa_*\mathcal{F} = 0$ for
% $p \geq 1$ (by  Lemma \ref{lemma-relative-affine-vanishing} and
% Morphisms, Lemma \ref{morphisms-lemma-morphism-affines-affine})
% and we've already observed the result holds for $p = 0$ in the first
% paragraph. Next, let $U \subset X$ be a quasi-compact open, $V \subset X$
% an affine open, and assume $P(U)$, $P(V)$, $P(U \cap V)$ hold.
% Let $a = f|_U$, $b = f|_V$, $c = f|_{U \cap V}$, and $g = f|_{U \cup V}$.
% Then for any quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_{U \cup V}$-module $\mathcal{F}$
% we have the relative Mayer-Vietoris sequence
% $$
% 0 \to
% g_*\mathcal{F} \to
% a_*(\mathcal{F}|_U) \oplus b_*(\mathcal{F}|_V) \to
% c_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U \cap V}) \to
% R^1g_*\mathcal{F} \to \ldots
% $$
% see Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-relative-mayer-vietoris}.
% By $P(U)$, $P(V)$, $P(U \cap V)$ we see that
% $R^pa_*(\mathcal{F}|_U)$, $R^pb_*(\mathcal{F}|_V)$ and
% $R^pc_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U \cap V})$ are all quasi-coherent.
% Using the results on quasi-coherent sheaves in
% Schemes, Section \ref{schemes-section-quasi-coherent}
% this implies that each of the sheaves
% $R^pg_*\mathcal{F}$ is quasi-coherent since it sits in the middle of a short
% exact sequence with a cokernel of a map between quasi-coherent sheaves
% on the left and a kernel of a map between quasi-coherent sheaves on the right.
% Whence $P(U \cup V)$ and the proof of (1) is complete."
\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:higher_direct_image, lem:higher_direct_image_affine_vanishing}
  Quasi-coherence is local on \(S\), and forming higher direct images commutes
  with restriction to open subschemes, so we may assume \(S\) is affine; then
  \(X\) is quasi-compact and quasi-separated. We argue by the induction principle
  for quasi-compact opens of a quasi-compact quasi-separated scheme: for a
  quasi-compact open \(U \subset X\) with \(a = f|_U\), let \(P(U)\) be the
  property that \(R^p a_*\mathcal{G}\) is quasi-coherent on \(S\) for every
  quasi-coherent module \(\mathcal{G}\) on \(U\) and every \(p \geq 0\); the
  desired statement is \(P(X)\).

  \emph{Affine base.} If \(U\) is affine then \(a\) is an affine morphism, so
  \(R^p a_*\mathcal{G} = 0\) for \(p \geq 1\) by the relative affine vanishing of
  Lemma~\ref{lem:higher_direct_image_affine_vanishing}, while
  \(R^0 a_*\mathcal{G} = a_*\mathcal{G}\) is quasi-coherent because the pushforward
  of a quasi-coherent sheaf along a quasi-compact quasi-separated morphism is
  quasi-coherent. Hence \(P(U)\) holds.

  \emph{Inductive step.} Let \(U \subset X\) be quasi-compact open, \(V \subset X\)
  affine open, and suppose \(P(U)\), \(P(V)\), \(P(U \cap V)\) hold. Writing
  \(a = f|_U\), \(b = f|_V\), \(c = f|_{U \cap V}\), \(h = f|_{U \cup V}\), the
  relative Mayer--Vietoris sequence
  \[
    0 \to h_*\mathcal{G} \to a_*(\mathcal{G}|_U) \oplus b_*(\mathcal{G}|_V)
      \to c_*(\mathcal{G}|_{U \cap V}) \to R^1 h_*\mathcal{G} \to \cdots
  \]
  exhibits each \(R^p h_*\mathcal{G}\) as an extension built from quasi-coherent
  sheaves \(R^p a_*(\mathcal{G}|_U)\), \(R^p b_*(\mathcal{G}|_V)\),
  \(R^p c_*(\mathcal{G}|_{U \cap V})\): it sits between the cokernel of a map of
  quasi-coherent sheaves and the kernel of a map of quasi-coherent sheaves, hence
  is quasi-coherent. Thus \(P(U \cup V)\) holds. By the induction principle
  \(P(X)\) holds, proving the lemma.
\end{proof}

\section{Vanishing of higher direct images along an affine morphism}

\begin{lemma}\leanok
[Relative affine vanishing]
  \label{lem:higher_direct_image_affine_vanishing}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.higherDirectImage_affine_eq_zero}
  \uses{def:higher_direct_image}
  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Tag 02KG (Lemma
  % `lemma-relative-affine-vanishing')
  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L180--185).
  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $f : X \to S$ be a morphism of schemes.
  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.
  % If $f$ is affine then $R^if_*\mathcal{F} = 0$ for all $i > 0$."
  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Tag 02KG (``Relative affine vanishing'').}
  Let \(f : X \to S\) be a morphism of schemes and \(\mathcal{F}\) a
  quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. If \(f\) is affine, then
  \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F} = 0\) for all \(i \geq 1\).
\end{lemma}

% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "According to
% Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-describe-higher-direct-images}
% the sheaf
% $R^if_*\mathcal{F}$ is the sheaf associated to the presheaf
% $V \mapsto H^i(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)})$.
% By assumption, whenever $V$ is affine we have that $f^{-1}(V)$ is
% affine, see Morphisms, Definition \ref{morphisms-definition-affine}.
% By Lemma \ref{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero} we conclude that
% $H^i(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)}) = 0$
% whenever $V$ is affine. Since $S$ has a basis consisting of affine
% opens we win."
\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:higher_direct_image}
  By Definition~\ref{def:higher_direct_image}, \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) is the
  sheafification of the presheaf \(V \mapsto H^i(f^{-1}(V),
  \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)})\). Since \(f\) is affine, the preimage \(f^{-1}(V)\) of
  any affine open \(V \subset S\) is affine. The higher cohomology of a
  quasi-coherent sheaf on an affine scheme vanishes, so
  \(H^i(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)}) = 0\) for all \(i \geq 1\) and all
  affine \(V\). As the affine opens form a basis of \(S\), the sheafification of a
  presheaf that vanishes on a basis is zero; hence \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F} = 0\) for
  all \(i \geq 1\).
\end{proof}

\section{Flat base change for the higher direct images}

\begin{theorem}\leanok
[Flat base change, $i \geq 1$ case]
  \label{thm:flat_base_change_higher}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.flatBaseChange_higherDirectImage_isIso}
  \uses{def:higher_direct_image, lem:higher_direct_image_quasi_coherent,
    def:pushforward_base_change_map}
  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Tag 02KH (Lemma "Flat base change"), the
  % $i \geq 1$ case (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L947--970, and
  % references/stacks-coherent.md).
  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Consider a cartesian diagram of schemes
  % $$
  % \xymatrix{
  % X' \ar[d]_{f'} \ar[r]_{g'} & X \ar[d]^f \\
  % S' \ar[r]^g & S
  % }
  % $$
  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module
  % with pullback $\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}$.
  % Assume that $g$ is flat and that $f$ is quasi-compact and quasi-separated.
  % For any $i \geq 0$
  % \begin{enumerate}
  % \item the base change map of
  % Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-base-change-map-flat-case}
  % is an isomorphism
  % $$
  % g^*R^if_*\mathcal{F} \longrightarrow R^if'_*\mathcal{F}',
  % $$
  % \item if $S = \Spec(A)$ and $S' = \Spec(B)$, then
  % $H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B = H^i(X', \mathcal{F}')$.
  % \end{enumerate}"
  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Tag 02KH (``Flat base change''), the
  \(i \geq 1\) case.}
  Consider the cartesian square
  \[
    \begin{array}{ccc}
      X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
      {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
      S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
    \end{array}
  \]
  with \(\mathcal{F}\) a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module and
  \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\). Assume that \(g\) is flat and that \(f\) is
  quasi-compact and quasi-separated. Then for every \(i \geq 1\) the canonical
  base-change map (the higher-degree analogue of the pushforward base-change map
  of Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map}) is an isomorphism
  \[
    g^*\bigl(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\bigr) \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
    R^i f'_*\bigl((g')^*\mathcal{F}\bigr) = R^i f'_*\mathcal{F}' .
  \]
  Equivalently, in the affine situation \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\),
  \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(B)\) with \(A \to B\) flat, the comparison map of
  \(B\)-modules
  \[
    H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
    H^i(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)
  \]
  is an isomorphism, where \(X_B = X \times_{\operatorname{Spec}(A)}
  \operatorname{Spec}(B)\) and \(\mathcal{F}_B\) is the pullback of
  \(\mathcal{F}\).
\end{theorem}

% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Having said this, part (1) follows from part (2). Namely,
% part (1) is local on $S'$ and hence we may assume $S$
% and $S'$ are affine. In other words, we have $S = \Spec(A)$
% and $S' = \Spec(B)$ as in (2).
% Then since $R^if_*\mathcal{F}$ is quasi-coherent
% (Lemma \ref{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images}),
% it is the quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_S$-module associated to the
% $A$-module $H^0(S, R^if_*\mathcal{F}) = H^i(X, \mathcal{F})$
% (equality by
% Lemma \ref{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images-application}).
% Similarly, $R^if'_*\mathcal{F}'$ is the quasi-coherent
% $\mathcal{O}_{S'}$-module associated to the $B$-module
% $H^i(X', \mathcal{F}')$. Since pullback by $g$ corresponds
% to $- \otimes_A B$ on modules
% (Schemes, Lemma \ref{schemes-lemma-widetilde-pullback})
% we see that it suffices to prove (2).
%
% Let $A \to B$ be a flat ring homomorphism.
% Let $X$ be a quasi-compact and quasi-separated scheme over $A$.
% Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.
% Set $X_B = X \times_{\Spec(A)} \Spec(B)$ and denote
% $\mathcal{F}_B$ the pullback of $\mathcal{F}$.
% We are trying to show that the map
% $$
% H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \longrightarrow H^i(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)
% $$
% (given by the reference in the statement of the lemma)
% is an isomorphism.
%
% In case $X$ is separated, choose an affine open covering
% $\mathcal{U} : X = U_1 \cup \ldots \cup U_t$ and recall that
% $$
% \check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = H^p(X, \mathcal{F}),
% $$
% see
% Lemma \ref{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent}.
% If $\mathcal{U}_B : X_B = (U_1)_B \cup \ldots \cup (U_t)_B$ we obtain
% by base change, then it is still the case that each $(U_i)_B$ is affine
% and that $X_B$ is separated. Thus we obtain
% $$
% \check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}_B, \mathcal{F}_B) = H^p(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B).
% $$"
\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:higher_direct_image, lem:higher_direct_image_quasi_coherent,
    def:pushforward_base_change_map}
  \emph{Reduction to the affine target.} The assertion is local on \(S'\), so we
  may assume \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) and \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(B)\) with
  \(A \to B\) flat. By quasi-coherence of the higher direct images
  (Lemma~\ref{lem:higher_direct_image_quasi_coherent}), \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) is
  the quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module associated to the \(A\)-module
  \(H^0(S, R^i f_*\mathcal{F}) = H^i(X, \mathcal{F})\), and likewise
  \(R^i f'_*\mathcal{F}'\) is associated to the \(B\)-module
  \(H^i(X', \mathcal{F}')\). Since pullback along \(g\) corresponds to
  \(- \otimes_A B\) on modules, the base-change map is identified with the
  comparison map of \(B\)-modules
  \[
    H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \longrightarrow H^i(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B),
    \qquad X_B = X \times_{\operatorname{Spec}(A)} \operatorname{Spec}(B),
  \]
  and it suffices to prove that this map is an isomorphism.

  separated over \(A\). Choose a finite affine open cover
  \(\mathcal{U} : X = U_1 \cup \dots \cup U_t\); by separatedness every finite
  intersection \(U_{i_0 \dots i_p}\) is again affine, so {\v C}ech cohomology
  computes sheaf cohomology:
  \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = H^p(X, \mathcal{F})\) for all \(p\).

  \(\mathcal{U}_B : X_B = (U_1)_B \cup \dots \cup (U_t)_B\) of the again-separated
  \(X_B\), with the same property

  affine case identifies each term of the base-changed {\v C}ech complex with the
  original term tensored over \(A\) with \(B\); that is, there is an isomorphism of
  cochain complexes
  \[
    \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}_B, \mathcal{F}_B) \;\cong\;
    \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B .

  commutes with the formation of cohomology of a complex; taking \(H^i\) in any
  degree \(i \geq 1\) gives the desired isomorphism
  \(H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \cong H^i(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)\).

  Choose a finite affine open cover \(\mathcal{U} : X = U_1 \cup \dots \cup U_t\).

  than affine, but they fall under the separated case already treated, so flat base
  change holds for each of them. Comparing the two {\v C}ech-to-cohomology spectral
  sequences with \(E_2\)-pages
  \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \underline{H}^q(\mathcal{F}))\) and
  \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}_B, \underline{H}^q(\mathcal{F}_B))\), converging to
  \(H^{p+q}(X, \mathcal{F})\) and \(H^{p+q}(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)\) respectively, the
  separated case provides the term-by-term identification
  \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}_B, \underline{H}^q(\mathcal{F}_B)) =
  \check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \underline{H}^q(\mathcal{F})) \otimes_A B\) on the
  \(E_2\)-pages; flatness of \(A \to B\) propagates this isomorphism through the
  spectral sequences to the abutment, giving
  \(H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \cong H^i(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)\) in every degree
  \(i \geq 1\). Translating back to sheaves on \(S'\) yields the asserted
  isomorphism \(g^*(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}) \xrightarrow{\sim} R^i f'_*\mathcal{F}'\).
\end{proof}

1546	The Quot functor is constructed as a locally closed subfunctor of the
1547	Grassmannian
1548	\(\mathrm{Grass}(W \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_S} \mathrm{Sym}^r V, \Phi(r))\), via:

1550	  \item the uniform \(m\)-regularity bound from

1557	    universal quotient on the Grassmannian, producing the locally closed

1559	  \item the valuative-criterion upgrade
1560	    (\cref{lem:quot_valuative_criterion}) to closed embedding, hence
1561	    projective \(S\)-scheme.

1565	subfunctors via the kernel-vanishing scheme.

232	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme and let \(V\) be a locally free
233	  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module of rank \(r\). For an integer \(1 \le d \le r\), the
234	  \emph{Grassmannian of rank-\(d\) quotients of \(V\)} is the functor
235	  \[
236	    \mathrm{Grass}(V, d) : (\mathrm{Sch}/S)^{op} \longrightarrow \mathbf{Set},
237	    \qquad
238	    T \longmapsto \{ \langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle :
239	    q : V_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F},\; \mathcal{F}
240	    \text{ locally free of rank } d \}
241	  \]
242	  with equivalence \(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\). Concretely \(\mathrm{Grass}(V, d) =
243	  \Quot^{d,\mathcal{O}_S}_{V/S/S}\) (the Quot functor for \(X = S\), \(E = V\),
244	  constant Hilbert polynomial \(\Phi = d\)).

230	  \cref{lem:lbc_trivializing_cover}, the restriction \(M|_{U_i}\) admits the
231	  trivial finite free presentation
232	  \[
233	    0 \;\longrightarrow\; \struct{U_i} \;\longrightarrow\; M|_{U_i}
234	    \;\longrightarrow\; 0,
235	  \]
236	  i.e. \(M|_{U_i}\) is the cokernel of the zero map
237	  \(\bigoplus_{j \in \varnothing} \struct{U_i} \to \struct{U_i}\)
238	  (the case \(m = 0\), \(n = 1\) of the finite-presentation pattern). The
239	  unit sheaf-as-module \(\struct{U_i}\) (Mathlib's \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\))
240	  carries a canonical free presentation on one generator with no relations;
241	  this presentation is finite.
242	\end{lemma}
243	
244	\begin{proof}
245	  \uses{lem:lbc_trivializing_cover}
246	  \leanok
247	  The structure sheaf-as-module \(\struct{U_i} = \mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\,
248	  (U_i).\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\) is free of rank one on the single generator
249	  \(1\): the identity map \(\struct{U_i} \to \struct{U_i}\) exhibits it as
250	  \(\bigoplus_{i \in \{*\}} \struct{U_i}\), so the surjection
251	  \(\struct{U_i}^{\oplus 1} \twoheadrightarrow \struct{U_i}\) is an isomorphism
252	  and its kernel is zero, generated by the empty family. Thus \(\struct{U_i}\)
253	  has the canonical presentation with \(n = 1\) generators and \(m = 0\)
254	  relations; this canonical \(\mathtt{unit}\)-presentation is the rank-one free
255	  presentation, and Mathlib records it as \emph{finite} (the
256	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\) finite-presentation instance).
257	
258	  The chart presentation is obtained by transporting the canonical finite
259	  presentation of \(\mathtt{unit}\,(U_i).\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\) along the
260	  isomorphism \(e_i\), using \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.Presentation.ofIsIso}\).
261	  This construction carries any finite presentation of a module to a finite
262	  presentation of any isomorphic module, so the transported presentation of
263	  \(M|_{U_i}\) is again finite.
264	  % NOTE. The over-restrict categorical bridge is the shared construction
265	  % `Scheme.Modules.chartOverIso` (lem:chart_over_iso,
266	  % chap:Picard_SheafOverEquivalence).
267	\end{proof}
268	
269	\section{Finite presentation of a locally trivial line bundle}
270	\label{sec:lbc_finite_presentation}
271	
272	\begin{theorem}\leanok
273	[Locally trivial \(\Rightarrow\) finitely presented]
274	  \label{thm:lbc_isFinitePresentation}
275	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial.isFinitePresentation}
276	  \uses{lem:lbc_trivializing_cover, lem:lbc_chart_presentation, rem:lbc_site_instances}
277	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces,
278	  % Lemma 17.25.4 (Tag 0B8M), invertible vs locally free of rank one
279	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L4159-L4165).
280	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
281	  % "Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space. Any locally free
282	  %  $\mathcal{O}_X$-module of rank $1$ is invertible.
283	  %  If all stalks $\mathcal{O}_{X, x}$ are local rings, then
284	  %  the converse holds as well (but in general this is not the case)."
285	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag~0B8M (Modules, Lemma~17.25.4).}
286	  Let \(X\) be a scheme satisfying \cref{rem:lbc_site_instances} and let
287	  \(M : X.\mathtt{Modules}\) be locally trivial, \(\texttt{IsLocallyTrivial}(M)\). Then
288	  \(M\) is finitely presented: \(M.\mathtt{IsFinitePresentation}\) holds in
289	  Mathlib's \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.IsFinitePresentation}\).
290	\end{theorem}
291	
292	\begin{proof}
293	  \uses{lem:lbc_trivializing_cover, lem:lbc_chart_presentation}
294	  \leanok
295	  By \cref{lem:lbc_trivializing_cover} choose a trivialising affine cover
296	  \(\{U_i\}_{i \in I}\) with isomorphisms \(e_i : M|_{U_i} \cong \struct{U_i}\).
297	  Assemble these into a \(\mathtt{QuasicoherentData}\) for \(M\): the index type
298	  is \(I\), the covering objects are the affine opens \(U_i\) (which cover \(X\)),
299	  and the presentation attached to index \(i\) is the finite free presentation of
300	  \(M|_{U_i}\) (equivalently, of the restriction \(M.\mathtt{over}\,U_i\))
301	  produced in \cref{lem:lbc_chart_presentation}. Each such presentation is
302	  finite by \cref{lem:lbc_chart_presentation}; the assembled
303	  \(\mathtt{QuasicoherentData}\) therefore satisfies
304	  \(\mathtt{QuasicoherentData.IsFinitePresentation}\), and the constructor
305	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.IsFinitePresentation.mk}\) yields
306	  \(M.\mathtt{IsFinitePresentation}\).
307	
308	  The only non-formal input is the trivialising cover; everything else is the
309	  definitional unwinding of finite presentation as a local property
310	  (Stacks, ``Modules of finite presentation''), since a rank-one free module is its own finite
311	  presentation. No stalk computation and no neighbourhood-spreading argument is
312	  used: the hypothesis already provides the local free models.
313	\end{proof}
314	
315	\begin{corollary}\leanok
316	[Locally trivial \(\Rightarrow\) finite type]
317	  \label{cor:lbc_isFiniteType}
318	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial.isFiniteType}
319	  \uses{thm:lbc_isFinitePresentation}
320	  Under the hypotheses of \cref{thm:lbc_isFinitePresentation}, the locally
321	  trivial module \(M\) is of finite type (\(M.\mathtt{IsFiniteType}\)). Quasi-coherence
322	  (\(M.\mathtt{IsQuasicoherent}\)) follows from finite presentation via
323	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.instIsQuasicoherentOfIsFinitePresentation}\).
324	\end{corollary}

390	  % "Dimension theory of finitely generated $k$-algebras").
391	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $k$ be a field. Let $S$ be a finite type $k$
392	  % algebra. Let $X = \Spec(S)$. Let $\mathfrak p \subset S$ be a prime
393	  % ideal, and let $x \in X$ be the corresponding point. Then we have
394	  % $\dim_x(X) = \dim(S_{\mathfrak p}) + \text{trdeg}_k\
395	  % \kappa(\mathfrak p)$."
396	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag~00OE (\(=\) \texttt{lemma-dimension-at-a-point-finite-type-field}).}
397	  Let \(k\) be a field, let \(S\) be a finite-type \(k\)-algebra, and let
398	  \(\mathfrak q \subset S\) be a prime ideal corresponding to a point
399	  \(x \in \mathrm{Spec}(S)\). Then
400	  \[
401	    \dim_x(\mathrm{Spec}\,S)
402	    \;=\; \dim S_{\mathfrak q} + \mathrm{trdeg}_k\,\kappa(\mathfrak q).
403	  \]
404	  In particular, if \(\mathfrak q\) is a maximal ideal (so \(x\) is a
405	  closed point) and \(k\) is algebraically closed, then
406	  \(\kappa(\mathfrak q) = k\) (Nullstellensatz), so
407	  \(\mathrm{trdeg}_k\,\kappa(\mathfrak q) = 0\) and
408	  \(\dim S_{\mathfrak q} = \dim_x(\mathrm{Spec}\,S)\).
409	
410	  \paragraph{Geometric application.} Applied to the iter-193 Stage~3
411	  setup --- \(k = \bar k\), \(S = \Gamma(X.\mathrm{left}, V)\) a
412	  standard-smooth \(\bar k\)-algebra of relative dimension \(n\) (Stage
413	  3), and \(\mathfrak q\) the prime defining the point \(z \in V\) --- the
414	  formula yields \(\dim(\mathcal O_{X, z}) = \dim S_{\mathfrak q} = n -
415	  \mathrm{trdeg}_{\bar k}\,\kappa(z)\). For \(z\) a closed point this is
416	  simply \(n\); for the generic point of a codim-\(1\) subvariety
417	  (\(\mathrm{trdeg} = n - 1\)) it is \(1\).
418	\end{lemma}
419

421	% we know that $r = \text{trdeg}_k\ \kappa(\mathfrak p)$ is equal to the
422	% dimension of $V(\mathfrak p)$. Pick any maximal chain of primes
423	% $\mathfrak p \subset \mathfrak p_1 \subset \ldots \subset \mathfrak p_r$
424	% starting with $\mathfrak p$ in $S$. This has length $r$ by Lemma
425	% \ref{lemma-dimension-spell-it-out}. Let $\mathfrak q_j$, $j \in J$ be
426	% the minimal primes of $S$ which are contained in $\mathfrak p$. These
427	% correspond $1-1$ to minimal primes in $S_{\mathfrak p}$ via the rule

429	% \ref{lemma-dimension-at-a-point-finite-type-over-field} we know that
430	% $\dim_x(X)$ is equal to the maximum of the dimensions of the rings

432	% $\mathfrak q_j \subset \mathfrak p'_1 \subset \ldots \subset
433	% \mathfrak p'_{s(j)} = \mathfrak p$. Then $\dim(S_{\mathfrak p}) =
434	% \max_{j \in J} s(j)$. Now, each chain
435	% $\mathfrak q_j \subset \mathfrak p'_1 \subset \ldots \subset
436	% \mathfrak p'_{s(j)} = \mathfrak p \subset \mathfrak p_1 \subset \ldots
437	% \subset \mathfrak p_r$ is a maximal chain in $S/\mathfrak q_j$, and by
438	% what was said before we have $\dim_x(X) = \max_{j \in J} r + s(j)$.
439	% The lemma follows."

449	  citation of the dimension theory of finitely generated algebras over a

451	  finite over a polynomial subalgebra, so the going-up / catenary
452	  dimension theory of such algebras applies. Write
453	  \(r = \mathrm{trdeg}_k\,\kappa(\mathfrak q)\); this transcendence
454	  degree is exactly the dimension of the closed subvariety

279	
280	\leanok

45:\begin{proof}
50:  \uses{lem:depth_short_exact_sequence, lem:auslander_buchsbaum_formula_succ_pd,

360	\begin{proof}
361	  
362	  
363	  
364	\leanok
365	  \uses{lem:depth_short_exact_sequence, lem:auslander_buchsbaum_formula_succ_pd,
366	    lem:depth_eq_of_linearEquiv, lem:depth_pi_const_eq_depth_of_nonempty}
367	  We induct on \(\text{depth}(M)\).
368	
369	  \emph{Base case \(\text{depth}(M) = 0\).} Let \(e := \text{pd}_R(M)\) and pick a minimal
370	  finite free resolution
371	  \[
372	    0 \longrightarrow R^{n_e} \longrightarrow R^{n_{e-1}} \longrightarrow
373	    \cdots \longrightarrow R^{n_0} \longrightarrow M \longrightarrow 0
374	  \]
375	  with all transition maps having matrix coefficients in \(\mathfrak{m}\) (this is the
376	  ``minimal-resolution-after-trimming-trivial-summands'' normalisation, cf.\ Stacks
377	  \texttt{lemma-add-trivial-complex}). On the one hand, the ``what is exact'' criterion
378	  (Stacks tag 00MF, \texttt{proposition-what-exact}, expressing exactness of a sequence of
379	  finite free modules in terms of depths of ideals of \(r\)-minors) gives
380	  \(\text{depth}(R) \geq e\). On the other hand, splitting the resolution into short exact
381	  sequences

381	  sequences
382	  \[
383	    0 \to R^{n_e} \to R^{n_{e-1}} \to K_{e-2} \to 0, \quad
384	    0 \to K_{e-2} \to R^{n_{e-2}} \to K_{e-3} \to 0, \quad \dots, \quad
385	    0 \to K_0 \to R^{n_0} \to M \to 0,
386	  \]
387	  and applying \cref{lem:depth_short_exact_sequence} iteratively,
388	  yields the chain of inequalities
389	  \(\text{depth}(K_{e-2}) \geq \text{depth}(R) - 1\),
390	  \(\text{depth}(K_{e-3}) \geq \text{depth}(R) - 2\), \dots,
391	  \(\text{depth}(K_0) \geq \text{depth}(R) - (e-1)\), and finally
392	  \(\text{depth}(M) \geq \text{depth}(R) - e\). Since \(\text{depth}(M) = 0\) we deduce
393	  \(\text{depth}(R) \leq e\). Combined with the previous inequality, $\text{depth}(R) = e =
394	  \text{pd}_R(M) + 0 = \text{pd}_R(M) + \text{depth}(M)$.
395	
396	  \emph{Inductive step \(\text{depth}(M) > 0\).} We claim there exists \(x \in \mathfrak{m}\)
397	  which is a non-zero-divisor on both \(R\) and \(M\). Indeed: either \(\text{pd}_R(M) > 0\), in
398	  which case \(\text{depth}(R) > 0\) by the ``what is exact'' criterion applied to the start
399	  of a minimal resolution, or \(\text{pd}_R(M) = 0\) in which case \(M\) is finite free and
400	  \(\text{depth}(R) = \text{depth}(M) > 0\). Either way both \(\text{depth}(R) > 0\) and
401	  \(\text{depth}(M) > 0\), hence \(\text{depth}(R \oplus M) > 0\) by
402	  \cref{lem:depth_short_exact_sequence}, so a common non-zero-divisor \(x \in
403	  \mathfrak{m}\) exists. Take a minimal finite free resolution of \(M\) as above; the snake
404	  lemma applied to multiplication by \(x\) gives a minimal finite free resolution
405	  \[
406	    0 \to (R/xR)^{n_e} \to (R/xR)^{n_{e-1}} \to \cdots \to (R/xR)^{n_0} \to M/xM \to 0
407	  \]
408	  of \(M/xM\) over \(R/xR\), of the same length \(e\). So
409	  \(\text{pd}_{R/xR}(M/xM) = \text{pd}_R(M)\). By Stacks
410	  \texttt{lemma-depth-drops-by-one}, both \(\text{depth}(R/xR) = \text{depth}(R) - 1\) and
411	  \(\text{depth}(M/xM) = \text{depth}(M) - 1\), and one verifies
412	  \(\text{depth}_R(M/xM) = \text{depth}_{R/xR}(M/xM)\) (regular sequences in \(\mathfrak{m}\)
413	  correspond to regular sequences in \(\mathfrak{m}/(x)\)). Apply the inductive hypothesis to
414	  \(M/xM\) over \(R/xR\):
415	  \[
416	    \text{pd}_{R/xR}(M/xM) + \text{depth}(M/xM) = \text{depth}(R/xR).
417	  \]
418	  Substituting yields
419	  \(\text{pd}_R(M) + (\text{depth}(M) - 1) = \text{depth}(R) - 1\), i.e.\
420	  \(\text{pd}_R(M) + \text{depth}(M) = \text{depth}(R)\), as required.

import json, collections
d=json.load(open('.leandag/dag.json'))
nodes={n['id']:n for n in d['nodes']}
fwd=collections.defaultdict(list)
indeg=collections.Counter()
for nid in nodes: indeg[nid]=0
for e in d['edges']:
    if e['from'] in nodes and e['to'] in nodes:
        fwd[e['from']].append(e['to']); indeg[e['to']]+=1
# Kahn topo sort
q=[n for n in nodes if indeg[n]==0]
seen=0
indeg2=dict(indeg)
from collections import deque
q=deque(q)
while q:
    x=q.popleft(); seen+=1
    for y in fwd[x]:
        indeg2[y]-=1
        if indeg2[y]==0: q.append(y)
print('topo-sorted', seen, 'of', len(nodes), '=> ACYCLIC' if seen==len(nodes) else '=> CYCLE PRESENT')
EOF
